question_text: We always see the same face of the Moon from Earth. Which of the following best explains why this happens?

options.label: A
options.text: The Moon does not rotate at all as it orbits Earth

options.label: B
options.text: The Moon's rotation period is the same as its orbital period, so the same side always faces Earth

options.label: C
options.text: Earth's gravity is so strong it prevents the Moon from rotating

options.label: D
options.text: The Moon orbits Earth much faster than it rotates

correct_answer: B) The Moon's rotation period is the same as its orbital period, so the same side always faces Earth
explanation: The Moon rotates once on its axis in approximately the same time it takes to orbit Earth (about 27.3 days). This is called tidal locking and is why we only ever see one hemisphere of the Moon from Earth—the near side. The far side of the Moon remains hidden from us.
